110.code: void BFS(struct ver  G[ ], int v)
110.reason: [Recursive BFS implementation is incorrect and leads to infinite recursion or stack overflow. BFS should be iterative using a queue, not recursive. Also, the logic inside incorrectly processes only one neighbor before recursing.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Recursive BFS implementation is incorrect and leads to infinite recursion or stack overflow. BFS should be iterative using a queue, not recursive. Also, the logic inside incorrectly processes only one neighbor before recursing.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Recursive BFS implementation is incorrect and leads to infinite recursion or stack overflow. BFS should be iterative using a queue, not recursive. Also, the logic inside incorrectly processes only one neighbor per call and then recurses on the next queue element without proper loop control.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Recursive BFS implementation is incorrect and leads to infinite recursion or stack overflow. BFS should be iterative using a queue, not recursive. Also, the logic inside incorrectly processes only one neighbor before recursing.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.14285714285714285 , reason:  [Recursive BFS implementation is fundamentally flawed: BFS should be iterative using a queue. This recursive approach does not correctly simulate BFS order and will cause stack overflow or incorrect traversal. Also, queue state ('first', 'last') is global and gets corrupted across calls.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [The BFS function is implemented recursively, which is incorrect. BFS should be iterative using a queue. This recursive approach will cause infinite recursion or stack overflow because it calls BFS again without proper base case or queue management. Also, it processes only one neighbor per call and doesn't traverse all reachable nodes correctly.]

124.reason: [This recursive call causes undefined behavior when 'first >= last' (empty queue), leading to accessing uninitialized memory in 'dui'. Also, BFS must not be implemented recursively like this.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This recursive call causes undefined behavior when 'first >= last' (empty queue), leading to accessing uninitialized memory in 'dui'. Also, BFS must not be implemented recursively like this.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This recursive call causes undefined behavior when 'first >= last' (i.e., queue is empty), leading to accessing uninitialized or invalid memory in 'dui'. Also, BFS must not be implemented recursively in this manner.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [This recursive call causes undefined behavior when 'first >= last' (empty queue), leading to accessing uninitialized/invalid array elements and infinite recursion.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.14285714285714285 , reason:  [Recursive call uses 'dui[first]' which may be out of bounds or uninitialized, leading to undefined behavior. Also, recursion depth equals number of vertices, which may exceed stack limit.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.3333333333333333 , reason:  [Recursive call to BFS with dui[first] may access uninitialized or out-of-bound array elements when the queue is empty (first >= last), leading to undefined behavior or infinite recursion.]

83.reason: [Logic flaw in insertion sort: It assumes list is sorted, but condition 'p->adj < avex' is always true in a sorted list, so this check is redundant and may skip correct insertion point. Should compare only with next node. Also, fails to handle case where avex equals existing node (though problem says no duplicate edges).]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.14285714285714285 , reason:  [Logic flaw in insertion sort: It assumes list is sorted, but condition 'p->adj < avex' is always true in a sorted list, so this check is redundant and may skip correct insertion point. Should compare only with next node. Also, fails to handle case where avex equals existing node (though problem says no duplicate edges).]

130.reason: [Because BFS is implemented recursively and uses global queue state, multiple calls (e.g., if graph were disconnected) would corrupt traversal. But problem states graph is connected, so this may not trigger error, but design is still wrong.]
